amr-bin-yahya: Amr bin Yahya al-Mazini was a Medinan scholar of the second generation (tabi'in) and a grandson of the companion Abu Sa'id al-Khudri. He is well known for narrating the hadith on wudu (ritual purification) from his father Yahya bin Umara, which became foundational in Islamic jurisprudence. Hadith scholars regarded him as trustworthy and precise. He transmitted from several companions and their successors, and his reports appear in the major hadith collections.
um-atiya: Um Atiya al-Ansariyyah, may Allah be pleased with her, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, and one of the women who participated in military expeditions as a nurse and caretaker. She narrated numerous important hadiths regarding the washing of the dead, women's prayer, and related rulings. Her narrations were transmitted by her son and are recorded in al-Bukhari and Muslim. She is considered one of the notable female companions in hadith transmission.
ammar: Ammar refers most commonly to Ammar bin Yasir, may Allah be pleased with him, though the name appears without a patronymic in some chains. Where context is unclear, this entry serves as a placeholder for a companion or successor by this name who transmitted prophetic traditions. His reports appear in the major hadith collections and pertain to various aspects of worship and conduct.
abdullah-bin-al-harith: Abdullah bin al-Harith bin Nawfal al-Hashimi was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, and a member of the Banu Hashim clan. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet as well as from senior companions including Umar and Uthman, may Allah be pleased with them. He served as a governor in Basra and was regarded as a reliable narrator. His reports are found in the major hadith collections.
zaid-bin-wahb: Zaid bin Wahb al-Juhani was a tabi'i (successor generation) scholar who traveled to Medina to meet the Prophet but arrived after the Prophet's passing, may Allah have mercy on him. He was a close associate of Ali bin Abi Talib, may Allah be pleased with him, and narrated from many companions including Hudhaifa, Abu Dharr, and Ibn Masud. He is considered highly reliable by hadith scholars and his narrations are found throughout the major collections.
abu-bakr-as-siddiq: Abu Bakr As-Siddiq, may Allah be pleased with him, was the closest companion and father-in-law of the Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, and the first Caliph of Islam. He was the first adult male to embrace Islam and accompanied the Prophet during the Hijra to Medina. Though his tenure as Caliph was brief (632–634 CE), he consolidated the Muslim community and initiated the compilation of the Quran. He narrated numerous hadiths that are recorded across all major collections.
uqba: Uqba, where referenced without further qualification in hadith chains, typically refers to Uqba bin Amir al-Juhani, may Allah be pleased with him, or occasionally to another companion of the same name. This entry serves as a general placeholder for narrators identified only as Uqba in a given chain. The most prominent narrator by this name is the companion Uqba bin Amir al-Juhani, whose narrations appear in all major collections.
yahya-bin-said: Yahya bin Sa'id al-Ansari was a distinguished tabi'i scholar and judge of Medina. He was one of the most relied-upon transmitters of his generation, narrating from Anas bin Malik, Sa'id bin al-Musayyib, and many other senior successors. Imam Malik and other major imams transmitted from him extensively. Hadith scholars unanimously praised his precision and integrity, and his narrations form a cornerstone of the Muwatta and the Sahihayn.
as-saib-bin-yazid: As-Saib bin Yazid, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, who lived to a very advanced age and was one of the last surviving companions in Medina. He was carried as an infant to perform Hajj with the Prophet, and later served as an administrator during the caliphates of Umar and Uthman. His narrations, particularly regarding practices he witnessed during the Prophet's time, are considered reliable and appear in the major collections.
fatima-bint-al-mundhir: Fatima bint al-Mundhir was a tabi'iyyah (female successor) and the wife of the great jurist Hisham bin Urwa. She was the granddaughter of the companion al-Zubayr bin al-Awwam, may Allah be pleased with him, and narrated hadiths from her grandmother Asma bint Abi Bakr, may Allah be pleased with her. She is cited as a trustworthy narrator and her transmissions appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and other major collections.
amr-bin-taghlib: Amr bin Taghlib, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him. He narrated a small number of hadiths, including a well-known report about the Prophet's distribution of wealth and the importance of satisfying hearts in Islam. His narrations are found in Sahih al-Bukhari. He is regarded as a reliable companion narrator.
aisha: A'isha bint Abi Bakr, may Allah be pleased with her, was the beloved wife of the Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, and one of the greatest scholars among his companions. She is the third-highest transmitter of hadith overall, narrating over two thousand reports preserved in all major collections. She was a leading authority on matters of worship, family law, and the Prophet's private conduct. After the Prophet's passing she served as a teacher to generations of Muslims for nearly fifty years.
amir-bin-rabia: Amir bin Rabi'a al-Anzi, may Allah be pleased with him, was one of the early companions who embraced Islam before the migration to Abyssinia. He participated in both Hijras and was among the most dedicated early Muslims. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet, peace be upon him, including well-known narrations on funeral prayers and greeting the deceased. His reports appear in the six major hadith collections.
kharija-bin-zaid-bin-thabit: Kharija bin Zaid bin Thabit was a prominent Medinan tabi'i and one of the seven great jurists of Medina known collectively as the Fuqaha' al-Sab'ah. He was the son of the famous companion and Quran scribe Zaid bin Thabit, may Allah be pleased with him, and inherited his father's mastery of Islamic law and Quran sciences. He is considered highly reliable by hadith scholars and his narrations appear in the major collections.
amir: Amir, where cited without further specification in hadith chains, most commonly refers to Amir bin Shurahbil al-Sha'bi, a leading tabi'i scholar and jurist of Kufa, or occasionally to a companion named Amir. Al-Sha'bi was renowned for his encyclopedic knowledge, legal acumen, and sharp memory. He narrated from hundreds of companions and is considered one of the most authoritative transmitters of the successor generation. His narrations appear throughout the major hadith collections.
musa-bin-uqba: Musa bin Uqba al-Asadi was a Medinan tabi'i scholar best known for his pioneering work in Islamic historiography, particularly his early Sira (biography of the Prophet). He was a freed slave of the household of al-Zubayr and narrated from major successors including Nafi' and Urwa bin al-Zubayr. Imam Malik praised his Sira highly. His hadith narrations appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and Muslim.
abu-humaid-al-saidi: Abu Humaid Al-Sa'idi, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, from the Ansar of Medina. He is particularly known for narrating detailed descriptions of the Prophet's prayer, which are cited as authoritative in books of jurisprudence. His narration describing the prayer postures step by step is preserved in Abu Dawud and other collections. He is regarded as a reliable and precise companion narrator.
ziyad-bin-jubair: Ziyad bin Jubair was a tabi'i narrator from Basra who transmitted from companions including Ibn Umar, may Allah be pleased with them. He is known for narrating reports related to prayer, Hajj, and trade. Hadith scholars considered him trustworthy. His narrations appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim as well as the Sunan collections.
abdur-rahman-bin-al-qasim: Abdur-Rahman bin al-Qasim bin Muhammad bin Abi Bakr was a leading Medinan tabi'i scholar and the primary transmitter of the jurisprudence of his father al-Qasim bin Muhammad, one of the seven great Medinan jurists. He transmitted extensively from his father, who took knowledge from his grandmother A'isha, may Allah be pleased with her. Imam Malik relied heavily on his narrations in the Muwatta. He is considered one of the most reliable Medinan authorities.
zaid-bin-aslam-from-his-father: Zaid bin Aslam transmitted a number of hadith reports from his father Aslam, a freed slave and close companion of Umar bin al-Khattab, may Allah be pleased with him. Aslam witnessed many events during Umar's caliphate and preserved important narrations. Zaid bin Aslam was himself a prominent tabi'i scholar, and his reports from his father provide a valuable link to the practices of the second caliph. Their combined chain appears in the Muwatta and other collections.
abdullah-bin-abu-qatada: Abdullah bin Abu Qatada was the son of the companion Abu Qatada al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, one of the Prophet's horsemen and a major narrator of hadith. Abdullah narrated from his father, transmitting important reports about prayer, fasting, and prophetic conduct. His narrations are found in Sahih Muslim and other major collections. He is considered a reliable member of the successor generation.
abu-humaid: Abu Humaid, referenced without a nisba in some chains, most commonly refers to Abu Humaid al-Sa'idi, a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, known for his detailed narration of the Prophet's prayer. Where the reference is ambiguous, this entry serves as a general placeholder. The narrations attributed to Abu Humaid in the canonical collections are authoritative and frequently cited in jurisprudential discussions.
thabit-al-bunani: Thabit bin Aslam al-Bunani was a distinguished tabi'i scholar from Basra, famous for his deep piety, lengthy prayers, and close association with the companion Anas bin Malik, may Allah be pleased with him. He is considered one of the most reliable transmitters from Anas and narrated an enormous number of hadiths from him. Imam Ahmad and other hadith masters praised him without reservation. His narrations are abundant in all major hadith collections.
yahya: Yahya, when cited without further specification, typically refers to one of several prominent transmitters: most often Yahya bin Sa'id al-Ansari, the authoritative Medinan tabi'i, or Yahya bin Sa'id al-Qattan, the celebrated Basran hadith critic. Both are highly reliable. This entry serves as a general reference; the specific identity depends on the chain context. Both scholars' narrations appear extensively throughout the canonical collections.
abdullah-bin-abu-mulaika: Abdullah bin Abi Mulaika al-Taymi was a tabi'i scholar of Mecca who served as a judge and muezzin during the caliphate of Ibn al-Zubayr. He narrated from many companions, including A'isha, Ibn Abbas, and Ibn Umar, may Allah be pleased with them. Hadith scholars considered him trustworthy and knowledgeable. His narrations appear in Sahih al-Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, and the Sunan collections.
abdullah-bin-abu-aufa: Abdullah bin Abi Awfa, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, and one of the last to pass away in Kufa. He participated in the pledge of Ridwan and many military campaigns. He went blind in his old age and continued to teach until late in his life, becoming an important source for companions' narrations in Kufa. His hadiths appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim.
uqba-bin-amir: Uqba bin Amir al-Juhani, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, who later served as the governor of Egypt. He was a skilled archer and transmitted many hadiths, including important narrations on prayer, archery, and conduct. He also memorized and transmitted poetry and was known for his eloquence. His narrations appear across all six major hadith collections.
anas-ra: Anas bin Malik, may Allah be pleased with him, was the personal servant of the Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, for ten years and one of the most prolific narrators of hadith in Islamic history. He transmitted over two thousand hadiths covering nearly every aspect of the Prophet's life, worship, and character. He lived to an advanced age and served as a primary source for the successor generation in Basra. His narrations are central to all major hadith collections.
abdullah-bin-kab-bin-malik: Abdullah bin Ka'b bin Malik was a tabi'i and the son of the famous companion Ka'b bin Malik, may Allah be pleased with him, one of the three companions who stayed behind from the Tabuk expedition and whose repentance is mentioned in the Quran. Abdullah narrated from his father and transmitted important reports about that well-known event as well as other aspects of prophetic practice. His narrations appear in the major hadith collections.
said-bin-zaid: Sa'id bin Zaid, may Allah be pleased with him, was one of the ten companions promised Paradise by the Prophet, peace be upon him, and an early convert to Islam. He was the husband of the Prophet's paternal aunt Fatima bint al-Khattab and participated in nearly all major military campaigns. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet and his narrations are preserved in the major collections. He is counted among the greatest companions.
samura: Samura bin Jundab, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, who embraced Islam at a young age. He is known for his large collection of narrations and served as a governor in Basra during the caliphate of Muawiyah, may Allah be pleased with him. He transmitted many hadiths covering a wide range of topics and his reports appear in the major hadith collections, including Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim.
abu-umama: Abu Umama al-Bahili, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, and one of the most prolific narrators from the Syrian region. He settled in Homs and later in Damascus, where he became an important transmitter of prophetic traditions to the people of Greater Syria. He lived to an old age and narrated widely, with his reports appearing in the six major hadith collections.
yali: Ya'li, likely referring to Ya'la bin Umaiya or Ya'la bin Murra, may Allah be pleased with them, were companions of the Prophet, peace be upon him, who narrated a number of hadiths. Ya'la bin Umaiya participated in several military campaigns and served as an administrator. His narrations appear in the major Sunan collections. This entry serves as a general reference where the full name is unspecified in the chain.
sahl-bin-abi-hathma: Sahl bin Abi Hathma, may Allah be pleased with him, was a young companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, from the Ansar of Medina. He is particularly known for narrating the detailed hadith regarding the blood money case (qasama) between the Ansar and the Jews of Khaybar, which is an important legal precedent. His narrations appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim. He is regarded as a reliable companion narrator.
malik-bin-sasaa: Malik bin Sasaa, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, and one of the narrators of the detailed account of the Night Journey (Isra wal-Mi'raj). His narration of this event is preserved in Sahih al-Bukhari and is one of the primary sources for the description of that miraculous journey. He is regarded as a reliable companion narrator, and his report on this topic is of great theological significance.
abdullah-bin-zama: Abdullah bin Zama'a al-Qurashi, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, whose narrations are found in the major hadith collections. He is known for transmitting reports related to the Prophet's conduct and rulings. He was among those who were present at the death of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq. His narrations are preserved in Sahih al-Bukhari and other collections.
abdullah-bin-amr-bin-al-as: Abdullah bin Amr bin al-As, may Allah be pleased with him, was one of the most prolific companions in terms of hadith narration, having been authorized by the Prophet to write down hadiths directly. He was the son of the famous general Amr bin al-As and was known for his great asceticism, extensive worship, and scholarly dedication. His written collection, known as al-Sahifa al-Sadiqa, is considered one of the earliest hadith compilations. His narrations are found in all major collections.
abu-masud: Abu Masud al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, whose full name was Uqba bin Amr, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, and one of the participants in the Second Pledge of Aqaba. He is known for narrating important hadiths on prayer, charity, and the characteristics of Islam's ease. He later settled in Kufa and became an important source for the people of Iraq. His narrations appear in all major hadith collections.
hudhaifa-bin-al-yaman: Hudhaifa bin al-Yaman, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, who held the unique distinction of being the keeper of the Prophet's secrets concerning the hypocrites (munafiqin). He is well known for narrating hadiths about fitna (tribulations) and the signs of the Last Hour, and the caliph Umar would consult him about hypocrites. He later settled in Kufa and became one of its most important scholars. His narrations appear in all major collections.
ubaidullah-bin-adi-bin-al-khiyar: Ubaidullah bin Adi bin al-Khiyar was a prominent tabi'i scholar of Medina from the Quraysh tribe. He narrated from several companions including Uthman bin Affan, Ali bin Abi Talib, and Abu Hurayra, may Allah be pleased with them. He is particularly known for his narration of the story of the hypocrite whom the Prophet ordered be killed. Hadith scholars considered him reliable and his narrations appear in the major collections.
qais-bin-abi-hazim: Qais bin Abi Hazim was a leading tabi'i scholar who had the distinction of being the only person to have narrated from each of the ten companions promised Paradise. He narrated from Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, Ali, and many other senior companions, may Allah be pleased with them all. He was a Kufan scholar of great standing and is considered among the most authoritative transmitters of the successor generation. His narrations appear in all major hadith collections.
usaid-bin-hudair: Usaid bin Hudair, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, and one of the chiefs of the Aws tribe of the Ansar in Medina. He participated in the Second Pledge of Aqaba and was among the most respected of the Ansar. He is known for his courage, his love of the Quran, and several narrations about the descent of divine peace and angels when reciting Quran. His reports appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim.
sufyan: Sufyan, cited without further specification, typically refers to one of two major hadith scholars: Sufyan al-Thawri (d. 161 AH), the Kufan imam and master of hadith criticism, or Sufyan bin Uyayna (d. 198 AH), the Meccan scholar and major teacher of Imam al-Shafi'i. Both are considered among the greatest authorities in hadith. This entry is a general placeholder; the specific Sufyan is determined by chain context. Their narrations appear throughout all major collections.
ammar-bin-yasir: Ammar bin Yasir, may Allah be pleased with him, was among the earliest converts to Islam and endured severe persecution alongside his parents. His mother Sumayya is considered the first martyr in Islam. The Prophet, peace be upon him, praised Ammar highly and declared that a rebellious group would kill him, which came to pass at the Battle of Siffin. He is regarded as one of the foremost companions and his narrations are found in all major hadith collections.
yazid-bin-abi-ubaid: Yazid bin Abi Ubaid was a freed slave (mawla) of Salama bin al-Akwa, may Allah be pleased with him, and a tabi'i narrator. He transmitted hadiths from Salama and from other companions, covering topics of prayer, fasting, and military campaigns. Hadith scholars considered him trustworthy and reliable. His narrations appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim among other collections.
zaid-bin-aslam: Zaid bin Aslam al-Adawi was a prominent Medinan tabi'i scholar and a freed slave of Umar bin al-Khattab, may Allah be pleased with him. He narrated from his father Aslam, from Umar's children and household, and from many companions including Jabir and Abu Hurayra. He was also known as a Quran exegete whose tafsir was transmitted by his son Abdur-Rahman. Imam Malik narrated from him extensively. His reports appear in the major hadith collections.
safwan-bin-yala-bin-umaiya: Safwan bin Ya'la bin Umaiya was the son of the companion Ya'la bin Umaiya, may Allah be pleased with him, and narrated hadiths from his father. Ya'la bin Umaiya was a companion who participated in military campaigns and served as an administrator. Safwan transmitted his father's narrations to the next generation, and his reports appear in the major Sunan and Sahih collections. He is considered a reliable tabi'i narrator.
safwan-bin-muhriz: Safwan bin Muhriz al-Mazini was a tabi'i scholar of Basra known for his piety, extensive worship, and weeping out of fear of Allah. He narrated from Imran bin Husayn and other companions, may Allah be pleased with them. Hadith scholars praised his reliability and sincerity. His narrations appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim, particularly regarding hadiths about accountability on the Day of Judgment.
yusuf-bin-mahak: Yusuf bin Mahak was a Meccan tabi'i scholar who narrated from several companions including A'isha, Abu Hurayra, and Ibn Abbas, may Allah be pleased with them. He is known for reporting how the Quran was originally arranged in the mushaf (codex). Hadith scholars considered him trustworthy. His narrations appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and the Sunan collections.
zirr-bin-hubaish: Zirr bin Hubaish al-Asadi was a prominent tabi'i scholar of Kufa known for his mastery of the Arabic language and the Quran. He narrated from companions including Ali bin Abi Talib, Uthman, Ibn Masud, and Ubayy bin Ka'b, may Allah be pleased with them. He was particularly known for his study of Quranic recitation directly from senior companions. He lived to an extremely old age and is considered a highly reliable narrator.
abdullah-bin-jafar: Abdullah bin Ja'far, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of Ja'far bin Abi Talib, the cousin of the Prophet who was martyred at the Battle of Mu'ta. He was raised in the household of the Prophet, peace be upon him, after his father's martyrdom, and is reported to have received the Prophet's blessings. He was known for his generosity and nobility. His narrations from the Prophet appear in the major hadith collections.
saud: Saud, referenced without further specification in some hadith chains, likely refers to a tabi'i or companion transmitter by this name. Without additional biographical details from the chain context, a precise identification is uncertain. This entry serves as a placeholder for narrators identified only as Saud in a given isnad. Readers should consult the specific chain for more precise identification.
al-araj: Al-A'raj, whose full name was Abdur-Rahman bin Hurmuz, was one of the leading tabi'i scholars of Medina and among the most prolific transmitters from Abu Hurayra, may Allah be pleased with him. He was also a master of the Quran and Arabic language. Imam al-Zuhri and Malik relied heavily on his narrations, and he is considered among the most authoritative of Medinan tabi'in. His reports appear extensively in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim.
ali-rad: Ali bin Abi Talib, may Allah be pleased with him, was the cousin and son-in-law of the Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, and the fourth Caliph of Islam. He was among the first to embrace Islam, fought in nearly every major battle alongside the Prophet, and was renowned for his knowledge, bravery, and eloquence. He narrated a large number of hadiths from the Prophet and is one of the most important companion narrators. His reports appear in all major hadith collections.
abu-sa-id-al-khudri-rad: Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, may Allah be pleased with him, was one of the most prolific narrators among the companions of the Prophet, peace be upon him, transmitting over a thousand hadiths. He was an Ansari companion who participated in twelve military campaigns and lived in Medina until late in his life, becoming one of the primary sources for prophetic traditions in that city. His narrations cover theology, worship, eschatology, and ethics. They appear in all major hadith collections.
ibn-abbas-raa: Ibn Abbas, may Allah be pleased with him, Abdullah bin Abbas was the cousin of the Prophet Muhammad, peace be upon him, and is known as the 'Scholar of the Ummah' (Habr al-Umma) for his extraordinary mastery of Quran, tafsir, and hadith. The Prophet supplicated for him to be given understanding of the religion, and he fulfilled that du'a magnificently. He narrated thousands of hadiths and his tafsir is among the most authoritative. His narrations appear in all major collections.
jabir-bin-samurah-ra: Jabir bin Samurah, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, and the son of Samurah bin Jundab's brother. He settled in Kufa and became one of its important sources of prophetic traditions. He narrated numerous hadiths on a wide range of topics including prayer, the characteristics of the Prophet, and eschatology. His narrations are found in Sahih Muslim and across the other major hadith collections.
zaid-bin-arqam-ra: Zaid bin Arqam, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, who participated in seventeen military campaigns. He is particularly known for narrating the famous hadith of Ghadir Khumm and various reports about the Prophet's family and companions. He settled in Kufa and became a major source for prophetic traditions in Iraq. His narrations appear in Sahih al-Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, and the Sunan collections.
wail-bin-hujr-ra: Wa'il bin Hujr, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, and a nobleman from Yemen who came to accept Islam. The Prophet honored him and prayed for blessings upon him and his descendants. He is particularly known for narrating detailed descriptions of the Prophet's prayer, including hand placement and recitation. His narrations on prayer are frequently cited in fiqh literature and appear in the major Sunan collections.
hudhaifah-ra: Hudhaifa bin al-Yaman, may Allah be pleased with him, was the trusted confidant of the Prophet, peace be upon him, who kept the list of hypocrites secret at the Prophet's request. He is renowned for his narrations concerning tribulations (fitan), the signs of the Last Hour, and the characteristics of the Dajjal. The caliph Umar and other companions would consult him on matters of hypocrisy. He settled in Kufa and his narrations appear in all major hadith collections.
abu-bakra-ra: Abu Bakra, may Allah be pleased with him, whose full name was Nufay bin al-Harith, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, who liberated himself from slavery in Taif and joined the Muslims. He settled in Basra and became one of the most important sources of prophetic traditions in that city, narrating over a hundred hadiths. He is known for his narrations on leadership, justice, and prophetic guidance. His reports appear in Sahih al-Bukhari and Sahih Muslim.
abdullah-bin-umar-ra: Abdullah bin Umar, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of the second Caliph Umar bin al-Khattab and one of the most prolific narrators of hadith, transmitting over two thousand reports. He was renowned for his meticulous following of the Prophet's Sunnah in every detail of life and was considered an authority in both hadith and Islamic law. He lived to an advanced age in Medina and remained a primary source for prophetic traditions for the successor generation. His narrations are central to all major collections.
abu-bakrah-ra: Abu Bakrah al-Thaqafi, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet who is also known as Nufay bin al-Harith al-Thaqafi. He settled in Basra after the Muslim conquests and narrated many important hadiths. He is known for the famous narration in which the Prophet said, 'Do not return to disbelief after me by striking each other's necks.' His reports appear in Sahih al-Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, and the Sunan collections.
maqil-bin-yasar: Ma'qil bin Yasar al-Muzani, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, from the Muzayna tribe. He participated in the pledge under the tree (Bay'at al-Ridwan) and in military campaigns. He is particularly known for narrating the famous hadith recommending the recitation of Surah Yasin for the dying. He settled in Basra and his reports appear in the major hadith collections including Abu Dawud and al-Tirmidhi.
ubadah-bin-as-samit: Ubada bin As-Samit, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, and one of the chiefs of the Khazraj tribe of the Ansar. He participated in the First and Second Pledges of Aqaba and is the narrator of the famous hadith on the conditions of the pledge of allegiance to the Prophet. He later served as a teacher and governor in Syria. His narrations appear in Sahih al-Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, and other collections.
abu-az-zubair: Abu az-Zubair Muhammad bin Muslim al-Makki was a tabi'i scholar of Mecca who was one of the primary transmitters from the companion Jabir bin Abdillah, may Allah be pleased with him. He narrated an enormous number of hadiths from Jabir and is the main source for many of Jabir's reports in Sahih Muslim. Hadith scholars discussed his narrations critically; Imam Muslim used him extensively while others were more cautious. His narrations appear prominently in the Sahihayn.
abu-abdur-rahman-as-sulami: Abu Abdur-Rahman Abdullah bin Habib al-Sulami was a prominent tabi'i scholar of Kufa renowned for his mastery of Quranic recitation. He learned the Quran directly from Uthman, Ali, Ibn Masud, and Zayd bin Thabit, may Allah be pleased with them, and taught it in Kufa for forty years during the caliphate of Ali and beyond. He narrated hadiths from several companions and is considered a highly reliable authority. His narrations appear in the major collections.
abu-thalabah-al-khushani: Abu Tha'laba al-Khushani, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, who participated in the Treaty of Hudaybiyya and the pledge under the tree. He settled in Syria after the Muslim conquests and became one of the important narrators of prophetic traditions in the region. He narrated hadiths on a variety of topics including hunting, food, and religious obligations. His reports appear in Sahih al-Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, and the Sunan.
fadalah-bin-ubaid: Fadalah bin Ubaid al-Ansari, may Allah be pleased with him, was a companion of the Prophet, peace be upon him, who later served as a judge and governor in Damascus under Muawiyah bin Abi Sufyan, may Allah be pleased with him. He narrated hadiths from the Prophet on a variety of topics. He was known for his piety and judicial wisdom. His narrations appear in the major Sunan collections and in some narrations of Sahih Muslim.
abdul-malik-bin-umair: Abdul-Malik bin Umair al-Lakhmi al-Qurashi was a long-lived tabi'i scholar of Kufa who narrated from many companions including Jabir bin Samurah, al-Mughira bin Shu'ba, and others. He was a skilled orator and narrator who lived into the era of the atba' al-tabi'in (the generation after the successors). Hadith scholars generally considered him trustworthy though they noted some issues with his memory in old age. His narrations appear in all major collections.
usamah-bin-zaid: Usamah bin Zaid, may Allah be pleased with him, was the son of Zaid bin Haritha, the Prophet's freed slave and beloved companion, and was himself deeply loved by the Prophet, peace be upon him, who called him 'the beloved son of the beloved.' He was appointed by the Prophet to lead a military expedition just before the Prophet's passing. He narrated numerous hadiths and is considered one of the noble companions. His reports appear in all six major hadith collections.
atiyyah: Atiyyah al-Awfi was a tabi'i scholar of Kufa who narrated from companions including Abu Sa'id al-Khudri and Ibn Abbas, may Allah be pleased with them. He is known for his tafsir narrations and his transmission of Abu Sa'id's reports. Some hadith scholars noted concerns about the reliability of some of his narrations, while others accepted him. His reports appear in the Sunan collections and are frequently cited in Quranic commentary literature.
